info.name: TrustGate + TrustGuard E2E
info.description:
End-to-end flows wiring TrustGate to TrustGuard through the `trustguard` plugin.

Two self-contained scenarios (run one folder with Collection Runner):
- **Flow - injection_protection (SQLi)**: `injection_protection` detector, SQL-injection KO, optional direct `/v1/guard` via `collector_key`.
- **Flow - prompt_guard (jailbreak)**: `prompt_guard` detector via NeuralTrust Firewall; requires `firewall_base_url` + `firewall_token`.

Shared setup per flow: TrustGate gateway/registry/consumer, TrustGuard platform tenant + collector (`metadata.gateway_id`), TrustGate `trustguard` plugin policy with `collector_id`.

Auth:
- TrustGate admin: `Authorization: Bearer {{tg_admin_token}}`.
- TrustGuard admin: `Authorization: Bearer {{admin_jwt}}` (auto-minted from `admin_jwt_secret` + `team_id`).
- Runtime TrustGate↔TrustGuard: server-side platform OAuth2 (`TRUSTGUARD_CLIENT_ID`/`SECRET` on TrustGate = `TRUSTGUARD_PLATFORM_CLIENT_ID`/`SECRET` on TrustGuard).

Variables: tg_base_url, tg_proxy_url, tg_admin_token, guard_base_url, admin_jwt_secret, team_id, openai_api_key; prompt_guard also needs firewall_base_url, firewall_token.

exec: // Auto-mint admin_jwt (HS256) from team_id + user_id + admin_jwt_secret for the TrustGuard admin API.
// If admin_jwt_secret is empty, the admin_jwt you set manually is used as-is.
var uid = pm.environment.get('user_id') || pm.collectionVariables.get('user_id'); if (uid) { claims.user_id = uid; }

item.name: Flow - injection_protection (SQLi)
item.description: TrustGate proxy + TrustGuard `injection_protection` detector. KO = SQL injection → 403. Includes optional folder for direct `/v1/guard` via `collector_key`.

item.name: 0 - Health
item.description: Sanity probes for both services before provisioning anything.

item.name: 1 - TrustGate config
item.description: Provision a TrustGate gateway with an OpenAI backend, an API-key credential and an inline consumer. The `trustguard` plugin policy is created in folder 2b after the TrustGuard collector exists.

item.name: 2 - TrustGuard config
item.description: Provision the TrustGuard side: a platform-scoped tenant, an injection_protection detector, an enforcing policy with a block rule, and a collector linked to the TrustGate gateway via metadata.gateway_id. Platform scope resolves the collector (and its tenant) from the gateway_id sent by TrustGate, so no per-deployment service client is needed.

exec: pm.test('created (201) or already exists (409)', function () { pm.expect([201, 409]).to.include(pm.response.code); });
pm.test('platform scope returns no service_client', function () { pm.expect(body.service_client).to.be.undefined; });

request.description: The team is taken from the admin JWT (team_id), never the body. One team owns one tenant, so this may 409 on a seeded environment; the rest of the folder still works against the team's existing tenant. scope=platform issues no service_client credentials.

metadata.gateway_id MUST equal the TrustGate gateway id captured in step 1.1. In platform scope TrustGuard resolves this collector (and its tenant) from the gateway_id that TrustGate sends in the guard request.

item.name: 2b - TrustGate plugin policy
item.description: Creates the TrustGate `trustguard` plugin policy after the TrustGuard collector exists so `settings.collector_id` can reference `{{guard_collector_id}}`, then attaches the policy and auth to the consumer.

item.name: 3 - Execute
item.description: Drive the TrustGate proxy plane and observe the TrustGuard decision. OK: a clean prompt passes the guard and the LLM provider answers (200). KO: a SQL-injection prompt makes TrustGuard return a block finding, so the proxy returns 403 before reaching the provider.

exec: pm.test('allowed by TrustGuard, provider answered (200)', function () { pm.response.to.have.status(200); });

header.description: Gateway discovery in header mode (default). In subdomain mode use Host {slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN> instead.

request.description: The trustguard plugin extracts the prompt, calls TrustGuard /v1/guard, which returns no block finding, and the request is forwarded to the LLM provider. Requires a real openai_api_key for the full 200 (the provider must answer). If you only want to prove the guard allowed the request without a real key, change the assertion to status != 403.

header.description: Gateway discovery in header mode (default). In subdomain mode use Host {slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN> instead.

request.description: The trustguard plugin extracts the prompt, calls TrustGuard /v1/guard, which detects the SQL injection and returns a block finding. The plugin then returns HTTP 403 and the upstream provider is never called (so a real OpenAI key is not required for this request).

item.name: 4 - External caller via collector_key (direct /v1/guard)
item.description: Demonstrates the non-gateway routing path: a service that is NOT a TrustGate gateway calls TrustGuard's /v1/guard directly, addressing the collector by its server-generated `collector_key`. Uses the collector's tenant-scoped service_client credentials returned at collector creation (scope=tenant).

exec: pm.test.skip('collector service_client credentials missing (run folder 2 first)');

request.description: OAuth2 client-credentials with scope=tenant using the collector-bound service_client. The token authorizes /v1/guard for external callers.

item.name: 2. KO - Guard by collector_key (SQL injection -> block)

exec: pm.test.skip('no service token (set platform credentials in step 4.1)');
pm.test('guard returns 200 on a decision', function () { pm.response.to.have.status(200); });

request.description: The request addresses the collector by collector_key (no gateway_id). TrustGuard resolves the collector, runs the enforcing policy, the injection_protection detector flags the SQL injection, and the response carries status=block.

exec: pm.test.skip('no service token (set platform credentials in step 4.1)');

request.description: Same collector_key with a benign prompt: no detector flags it, so the guard does not block.

item.name: Flow - prompt_guard (jailbreak)
item.description: TrustGate proxy + TrustGuard `prompt_guard` detector (NeuralTrust Firewall). Requires `firewall_base_url` and `firewall_token`. KO = jailbreak prompt → 403.

item.name: 0 - Health
item.description: Sanity probes for both services before provisioning anything.

item.name: 1 - TrustGate config
item.description: Provision a TrustGate gateway with an OpenAI backend, an API-key credential and an inline consumer. The `trustguard` plugin policy is created in folder 2b after the TrustGuard collector exists.

item.name: 2 - TrustGuard config (prompt_guard)
item.description: Provision the TrustGuard side: platform-scoped tenant, `prompt_guard` detector (NeuralTrust Firewall jailbreak scoring), enforcing policy with a block execution rule, and a collector whose metadata.gateway_id matches the TrustGate gateway id.

exec: pm.test('created (201) or already exists (409)', function () { pm.expect([201, 409]).to.include(pm.response.code); });
pm.test('platform scope returns no service_client', function () { pm.expect(body.service_client).to.be.undefined; });

request.description: The team is taken from the admin JWT (team_id), never the body. One team owns one tenant, so this may 409 on a seeded environment; the rest of the folder still works against the team's existing tenant. scope=platform issues no service_client credentials.

exec: pm.test.skip('set firewall_base_url and firewall_token (NeuralTrust Firewall credentials for prompt_guard)');

request.description: prompt_guard calls the NeuralTrust Firewall jailbreak API. Per-detector credentials override the server global firewall config.

metadata.gateway_id MUST equal the TrustGate gateway id captured in step 1.1. In platform scope TrustGuard resolves this collector (and its tenant) from the gateway_id that TrustGate sends in the guard request.

item.name: 2b - TrustGate plugin policy
item.description: Creates the TrustGate `trustguard` plugin policy after the TrustGuard collector exists so `settings.collector_id` can reference `{{guard_collector_id}}`, then attaches the policy and auth to the consumer.

item.name: 3 - Execute (OK / KO jailbreak)
item.description: Drive the TrustGate proxy and observe TrustGuard prompt_guard decisions. OK: benign prompt passes (200 from provider when openai_api_key is valid). KO: jailbreak prompt triggers a block finding and TrustGate returns 403 without calling the provider.

exec: pm.test('allowed by TrustGuard, provider answered (200)', function () { pm.response.to.have.status(200); });

header.description: Gateway discovery in header mode (default). In subdomain mode use Host {slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN> instead.

request.description: Benign prompt: prompt_guard scores below threshold, TrustGuard allows, TrustGate forwards to OpenAI. Requires a valid openai_api_key for HTTP 200; the guard itself is proven if status is not 403.

exec: pm.test('blocked by TrustGuard prompt_guard (403)', function () { pm.response.to.have.status(403); });

header.description: Gateway discovery in header mode (default). In subdomain mode use Host {slug}.<GATEWAY_BASE_DOMAIN> instead.

request.description: Jailbreak prompt: prompt_guard scores above threshold via NeuralTrust Firewall, TrustGuard returns status=block, TrustGate trustguard plugin responds 403. No OpenAI key required.
